content.text: A model comparison scorecard is a structured evaluation framework that scores candidate LLMs across technical quality, enterprise requirements, and cost dimensions on a consistent scale, enabling fair comparison across vendors and guiding procurement decisions. Without a scorecard, model selection defaults to marketing claims, benchmark leaderboards that do not reflect real workload characteristics, or the model a team happened to try first.
What it is

A model comparison scorecard is a weighted matrix that maps a set of evaluation criteria to numerical scores for each candidate model. The criteria are divided into three categories: technical quality (tool-use accuracy, multi-step reasoning, instruction following, latency, context fidelity), enterprise requirements (data residency, compliance certifications, rate limits, model version stability), and commercial terms (cost per token, contract flexibility, support SLA). Each criterion is assigned a weight reflecting its importance for the organization's specific use cases.

The scorecard is completed through a combination of internal evaluation runs (running your actual workload against each candidate using an eval harness) and due diligence questionnaires (asking vendors to document their compliance certifications, SLAs, and terms). The output is a ranked weighted score per model that feeds directly into the procurement decision.
Why it matters

LLM quality varies dramatically by task type. A model that scores highest on general reasoning benchmarks may perform poorly on tool-use tasks that require precise JSON output, correct parameter inference, and graceful error recovery. Public benchmark leaderboards (MMLU, HELM, BIG-bench) measure general reasoning, not agentic tool-use quality — the specific capability that matters most for enterprise agent deployments.

Enterprise organizations also face requirements that are invisible in technical benchmarks: HIPAA compliance for healthcare, FedRAMP for government, data residency for EU/GDPR environments. A model that scores 95/100 on tool-use accuracy but cannot be deployed in the required region is not a viable option. The scorecard makes these requirements explicit criteria rather than after-the-fact surprises.
Benefits
Objective comparison: replaces subjective impressions with quantified scores on criteria that matter for the actual use case.
Stakeholder alignment: the scoring criteria and weights can be agreed upon before evaluation, ensuring all stakeholders accept the methodology before seeing results.
Audit trail: scorecard documentation provides a defensible record of why a specific model was selected, important for procurement compliance and future review.
Criteria completeness: the scorecard structure forces evaluation teams to consider enterprise requirements (compliance, residency, SLAs) alongside technical quality.
Reusability: a well-structured scorecard can be reused with updated results as new model versions are released or as requirements change.
Vendor negotiation: documented scorecard results give procurement teams concrete data points when negotiating pricing and SLAs with vendors.
Trade-offs and limitations
Weight subjectivity: the choice of criterion weights is inherently subjective. For example, weighting cost at 40% vs. 20% can reverse the ranking. Document the weight rationale explicitly.
Eval harness construction cost: building a representative eval harness for tool-use tasks requires significant engineering effort — low-quality evals produce misleading scores.
Static snapshot: scorecards reflect model capabilities at evaluation time. Models are updated frequently; a score that was accurate 6 months ago may not be today.
Gaming risk: vendors who see the scorecard criteria will optimize their models for those specific benchmarks. Use a mix of public and private evaluation tasks.
Diminishing returns at the top: if two models score 87 and 89 out of 100, the difference may not be practically meaningful — treat close scores as functionally equivalent.
How it works

The scorecard process has three phases. First, define criteria and weights: the evaluation team (including engineering, legal, security, and procurement) agrees on which criteria matter, their relative weights, and the scoring rubric for each criterion (what does a 1, 3, or 5 mean for each criterion). This agreement must happen before any model is evaluated to prevent motivated reasoning.

Second, run evaluations: for technical criteria, execute the eval harness against each candidate model using a representative sample of real production tasks. For enterprise criteria, send a standardized questionnaire to each vendor and verify responses against public documentation (SOC2 reports, DPA templates, public pricing pages). Record raw results in a shared spreadsheet.
Scoring rubric for tool-use accuracy

Tool-use accuracy is measured on a held-out test set of 200+ tasks representative of your actual agent workflows. Each task has a ground-truth tool call (function name + parameters) and a set of acceptable alternatives. Scoring: 5 = correct tool and parameters on first attempt, 4 = correct tool with minor parameter variation within tolerance, 3 = correct tool but requires one retry, 2 = wrong tool, 1 = no tool call when one was required, 0 = invalid output format.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Model comparison scorecard: technical evaluation dimensions and enterprise criteria

content.text: The scorecard evaluates candidates across two main axes: technical quality (tool-use accuracy, reasoning, latency, cost) and enterprise readiness (data residency, compliance, contract terms). Technical scores come from an internal eval harness run against each vendor's API. Enterprise scores come from vendor questionnaires and independent verification of compliance certifications. Both axes produce a 0-5 score per criterion; the weighted average determines the final ranking.
When to use it
Initial model procurement: use the full scorecard when selecting the primary and fallback model for a new agentic system.
Vendor contract renewal: re-run the evaluation before renewing a multi-year contract to confirm the incumbent still provides best value.
New use case expansion: run a targeted scorecard (focused on the new use case's requirements) when expanding to a new task type or compliance environment.
Competitive evaluation: run a lightweight scorecard (technical criteria only) quarterly to monitor whether new model releases from competing vendors represent a meaningful quality delta.
How to implement
Define the use case context: what tasks will the model perform, what response format is required, what are the latency constraints, what data classification applies to the inputs.

Assemble the evaluation team: at minimum, a technical evaluator (who builds the eval harness), a security/compliance reviewer (who verifies enterprise criteria), and a procurement representative (who negotiates commercial terms).
Define criteria and weights before evaluation: document each criterion, the scoring rubric (what constitutes a 1, 3, and 5), and the weight. Have all stakeholders sign off before running any models.

Build the eval harness: 200+ representative tasks with ground-truth annotations for tool-use accuracy, instruction following, and multi-step reasoning. Use a held-out test set not exposed to the vendor.
Run technical evaluations: execute the harness against each model API at the expected token count and request volume. Capture p50 and p99 latency, cost per 1K tasks, and error rates.

Run enterprise due diligence: send a standardized questionnaire to each vendor covering data residency, compliance certifications, rate limits, model version pinning, SLA terms, and contract exit clauses.
Compute weighted scores, rank models, and document the decision rationale including any tie-breaking reasoning for close scores.
Examples

# model-comparison-scorecard.yaml — Example completed scorecard
evaluation_date: "2025-07-01"
use_case: "Agentic customer support with tool use"

weights:
  tool_use_accuracy: 0.30
  multi_step_reasoning: 0.15
  latency_p99: 0.15
  cost_per_1m_tokens: 0.15
  data_residency: 0.10
  compliance_certs: 0.10
  model_version_stability: 0.05

models:
  - name: "Vendor A (claude-3-5-sonnet)"
    scores:
      tool_use_accuracy: 4.8
      multi_step_reasoning: 4.5
      latency_p99: 3.5   # p99 = 4.2s (target < 3s)
      cost_per_1m_tokens: 3.0
      data_residency: 5.0
      compliance_certs: 4.5
      model_version_stability: 5.0
    weighted_total: 4.22

  - name: "Vendor B (gpt-4o)"
    scores:
      tool_use_accuracy: 4.2
      multi_step_reasoning: 4.3
      latency_p99: 4.5
      cost_per_1m_tokens: 3.5
      data_residency: 4.0
      compliance_certs: 4.5
      model_version_stability: 3.5
    weighted_total: 4.08

decision: "Vendor A selected. Higher tool-use accuracy (+0.6) outweighs latency gap (0.7s p99)."
Real-world example

A logistics company evaluating models for an order-management agent had initially favored a model that topped general reasoning leaderboards. When they ran their tool-use eval harness — 300 tasks representing real order queries, inventory lookups, and delivery rescheduling requests — the leaderboard leader scored only 3.1/5 on tool-use accuracy (38% perfect rate), primarily because it tended to generate parameters in the wrong format for their JSON schema. A model that ranked lower on general benchmarks scored 4.4/5 (71% perfect rate) on the same tasks because it more reliably adhered to the function call schema.

The company also discovered during enterprise due diligence that the leaderboard leader did not offer a model version pinning option at their contract tier, meaning their agent could break silently after a model update. This criterion alone disqualified the leading technical performer from production consideration. The scorecard structure made this decision defensible to engineering leadership who had assumed the leaderboard leader was the obvious choice.
Common mistakes
Using public benchmark scores instead of running your own eval harness: public benchmarks measure general reasoning, not tool-use quality on your actual tasks.
Defining weights after seeing results: if weights are set after scores are computed, they will be rationalized to support the preferred outcome.
Ignoring latency and cost as evaluation dimensions: a model with 95/100 quality but 10-second p99 latency and 5x the cost of alternatives is not the right choice.
Not verifying vendor compliance claims: self-reported compliance certifications must be verified against the actual SOC2 report or third-party audit documentation.
Running evaluations at non-representative scale: latency and error rate numbers at 1 RPS will not predict behavior at 100 RPS with rate limit pressure.
Best practices
Use at least 200 evaluation tasks to get statistically reliable accuracy scores — 20-task evals have enormous variance and should not drive procurement decisions.
Include failure mode diversity in the eval harness: tasks where the correct answer is to not use any tool, tasks with ambiguous inputs, and tasks that require tool chaining.
Re-evaluate every 6 months: model capabilities evolve rapidly and the best-performing model at procurement time may no longer be the best choice 12 months later.
Document the scorecard rationale and store it alongside the procurement contract for future reference and audit defense.
Pilot the top-2 scoring models in a limited production environment before final selection — eval harness performance does not always predict production behavior under real user load.
Performance and scalability

The eval harness should run at production-representative request rates, not sequentially. Sequential evaluation of 200 tasks at 1-2 seconds per completion takes 3-6 minutes; at representative concurrency levels (10-20 simultaneous requests), it completes in under 60 seconds. More importantly, sequential evaluation will not reveal rate limit behavior, error rates under load, or connection pool exhaustion that appear in production.

For large-scale procurement decisions affecting multiple use cases (e.g., selecting a primary model for an organization with 20 different agent types), consider running separate scorecards per use case cluster rather than one aggregated scorecard. A model that excels at customer support tasks may perform differently on code generation or document analysis tasks.

Notes

Store test cases, runs, and score history in one place

Good starting point for tool-use and instruction-following tasks

Full control; easy to integrate with CI

Vendor due diligence templates

Standardized questionnaire sent to each vendor
Procurement contract management

Track renewal dates, version pin options, data rights
Comparison with alternatives
Evaluation approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Weighted scorecard with internal eval harness
Representative, comprehensive, defensible
High setup cost; requires eval harness engineering
Public benchmark leaderboard
No setup cost; regularly updated
Measures general reasoning, not tool-use quality
Informal team prototype testing
Fast; real developer intuition
Not reproducible; susceptible to first-impressions bias
Vendor-provided evaluation results
No engineering cost
Obvious conflict of interest; cannot validate methodology
A/B test in production
True production signal
Requires production traffic; risky for initial selection
Security and reliability

Evaluation tasks may contain sensitive business logic that reveals proprietary workflows, customer data structures, or internal system names. Use synthetic or anonymized data for all eval tasks, even those designed to represent real workloads. Store evaluation results in access-controlled storage — the scorecard documents your technical assessment of each vendor's weaknesses, which should not be shared externally.

For reliability: run the eval harness three times per model and report the median score rather than a single run. Single-run variance can be 5-10% for stochastic models at moderate temperature, which can move a close decision. Use temperature=0 for deterministic evaluation where the model supports it.
Key takeaways

Model comparison scorecards provide an objective, weighted evaluation across technical quality, enterprise requirements, and commercial terms — replacing benchmark leaderboards and informal impressions.
Tool-use accuracy measured on your actual task distribution is the most important technical criterion for agentic systems — public benchmarks do not measure this.
Define criteria weights before running any evaluation to prevent motivated reasoning from influencing the methodology.
Enterprise criteria (data residency, compliance certifications, model version pinning) are binary disqualifiers, not nice-to-haves — check them first.
Run the eval harness at production-representative concurrency to capture rate limit behavior and error rates under load.
Re-evaluate every 6 months: model capabilities evolve faster than procurement cycles, and the best model today may not be the best choice at renewal.
Pilot the top-2 scorecards in limited production before final selection — eval performance does not always predict production behavior under real user load.
